title: 5 Open Source Repos That Make Claude Code UNSTOPPABLE (March 2026)
description:
⚡Master Claude Code, Build Your Agency, Land Your First Client⚡
https://www.skool.com/chase-ai

🔥FREE community🔥
https://www.skool.com/chase-ai-community/

💻 Need custom work? Book a consult 💻
https://chaseai.io

It feels like every day there's a new open source GitHub repo changing the way we use Claude Code. So to cut through the noise, in this video I'm breaking down 5 of my favorite open source repos that all came out this month. We start with Karpathy's AutoResearch - the hottest repo on GitHub right now - and I give you the honest breakdown of what it can actually do versus what people are overhyping it for (hint: if a machine can't score it with a yes or no, the loop is just vibes). Then we get into OpenSpace, CLI-Anything, Claude Peers MCP, and Google Workspace CLI - all of which integrate with Claude Code and all launched in March 2026.

---

Repos talked about in this video:

AutoResearch - https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch
OpenSpace - https://github.com/HKUDS/OpenSpace
CLI-Anything - https://github.com/HKUDS/CLI-Anything
Claude Peers MCP - https://github.com/louislva/claude-peers-mcp
Google Workspace CLI - https://github.com/googleworkspace/cli

0:00 - Intro
0:20 - Autoresearch
6:12 - OpenSpace
9:23 - CLI Anything
10:35 - Claude Peers
12:52 - GWS
15:01 - More Resources

transcript: Every week, developers release new tools and frameworks as open-source GitHub projects. In this weekly update, we look at top trending open-source GitHub projects this week, so you can quickly see what developers are building. These projects highlight practical tools for AI workflows, automation, developer tooling, and local applications. Without wasting time, let's get started. Before we jump into today's project updates, here's a quick announcement for everyone. We've launched a brand new YouTube channel called AI Agent Studio dedicated entirely to AI Agent projects, tutorials, and tools. So, if you're interested in staying up to date with the latest AI Agent open-source projects, learning how to build your own agents, or exploring cuttingedge agent frameworks, make sure to check it out. Subscribe now to get weekly videos, in-depth guides, and realtime project breakdowns. The link is right there in the description. Don't miss it. All right, let's get into today's video. >> Project number one, Fish Speech. Open- source framework for expressive speech generation. Fish Speech is an open-source texttospech framework designed to generate natural and expressive speech using modern neural models. It provides tools to run speech synthesis models locally and supports workflows for generating voices from text prompts. The system processes text input, feeds it through trained speech models, and produces audio output that captures tone and rhythm. Developers run inference through Python tools and model checkpoints provided in the repository. This helps researchers and developers build voice applications and speech experiments. Explore the repository to generate speech locally. Project number two, Claude Code Plugins Directory. Official plug-in collection for Claude Code tools. Claude Code plugins directory is an open-source repository that lists official plugins designed to extend Claude code. It acts as a centralized catalog where developers discover integrations that add new capabilities to Claude's coding environment. Each plug-in connects Claude Code with external tools or services and exposes functionality that the assistant can use while helping with development tasks. The repository documents available plugins and how they integrate with the Cloud Code workflow. This helps developers expand the assistance capabilities inside their coding environment. Browse the repository to explore available plugins. Project number three, Astrobot platform for building multiplatform AI chat bots. Astrobot is an open-source framework that helps developers build and run AI chat bots across messaging platforms. It provides a runtime that connects language models with messaging services and plugins so bots can respond to user messages and perform actions. Developers configure models, install extensions, and connect supported platforms to run bots that manage conversations and automated responses. The system supports modular plugins that extend functionality for tasks and integrations. This helps developers create AI powered bots for communities and services. Explore the repository to deploy a chatbot platform. Project number four, Hindsight, framework for building retrieval systems from application logs. Hindsight is an open-source tool that builds retrieval systems using real application logs and production data. It collects user interactions and queries, processes them into structured data sets, and uses them to improve search and retrieval quality. Developers connect their application logs, generate training data, and evaluate retrieval performance through built-in workflows. The system focuses on improving retrieval pipelines used in Rag applications. This helps developers build search systems that reflect real user behavior. Explore the repository to turn application logs into retrieval data sets. Project number five, light RT lightweight runtime for ondevice machine learning. Lite RT is an open-source machine learning runtime designed to run models efficiently on edge and mobile devices. It provides optimized execution for neural network models while focusing on low latency and reduced resource usage. Developers integrate the runtime into applications that run models locally on devices such as phones or embedded systems. The system loads trained models and executes inference directly on the device without relying on cloud infrastructure. This helps developers deploy AI features where connectivity or compute is limited. Explore the repository to run ML models on device. Project number six, AI Hedge Fund simulation environment for AIdriven trading strategies. AI hedge fund is an open-source project that demonstrates how AI agents can collaborate to simulate hedge fund style investment strategies. It organizes multiple AI roles that analyze financial data, discuss trading ideas, and generate portfolio decisions within a structured workflow. The system runs locally and uses language models to coordinate reasoning and communication between agents. Developers experiment with prompts, data inputs, and decision rules to observe how agents approach market analysis. This helps researchers study multi-agent financial reasoning. Explore the repository to experiment with AIdriven trading simulations. Project number seven, Deerflow 2.0's framework for building AI workflow pipelines. Deerflow is an open-source workflow framework designed to orchestrate complex AI pipelines. It provides components that connect language models, data processing steps, and external tools into structured execution flows. Developers define tasks and dependencies. Then the system runs them in sequence or parallel to complete AI workflows. The framework manages data passing, execution state, and tool integration across the pipeline. This helps teams build structured AI systems that involve multiple processing stages. Explore the repository to build modular AI workflows. Project number eight, Notebook LM-PI Python interface for interacting with Notebook LM. Notebook LM-PI. PI is an open-source Python client that provides programmatic access to Notebook LM style workflows. It allows developers to send documents and queries through Python code and retrieve structured responses generated by language models. The library manages request formatting and interaction with the notebook LM service so developers can integrate document-based reasoning into applications. This helps developers automate workflows that involve summarizing, analyzing, or querying document collections. [music] Explore the repository to experiment with notebook LM through Python. Project number nine, Pi Multipass. Multipass image rendering pipeline for Raspberry Pi. Pi Multipass is an open-source rendering pipeline designed for Raspberry Pi devices that processes images through multiple sequential passes. It applies layered processing steps to generate visual outputs while working within the limited resources of embedded hardware. Developers run the system on Raspberry Pi hardware and configure processing stages that transform images step by step. The pipeline structure allows experimentation with different rendering or visual processing effects. This helps developers explore graphics and image processing workflows on small devices. [music] Explore the repository to experiment with multipass rendering. Project number 10, Emolunk, second screen dashboard system for game emulators. Emolunk is an open-source Android companion app that connects game emulators to a second screen and displays live gameplay data as themed dashboards or overlays. It communicates with supported emulator forks through a UDP protocol, reads game memory in real time, and converts the data into JSON that drives HTML, CSS, and JavaScript themes rendered inside a web view. Developers or theme creators define profiles that map memory values to visual components, allowing dashboards, widgets, or overlays to update continuously while a game runs. This helps emulator users build dynamic second screen interfaces and custom game overlays. Explore the repository to create your own emulator dashboards. Project number 11, Docs 4TS. Generate TypeScript documentation from source types. Docs4ts is an open-source documentation tool that generates structured documentation directly from Typescript source code. It analyzes TypeScript types, interfaces, and definitions to produce documentation that reflects the actual structure of the codebase. Developers run it on a project to extract type information and transform it into readable documentation files that stay consistent with the source code. The tool integrates into TypeScript development workflows and helps maintain accurate documentation without manual writing. This helps teams document libraries and APIs more reliably. Explore the repository to generate docs from your TypeScript types. Project number 12, MDL markdown processing toolkit for modern web projects. MDSilla is an open-source markdown processing toolkit designed to transform Markdown content into structured outputs for web applications. It parses Markdown files and converts them into formats that can integrate with modern web frameworks and content pipelines. Developers use it to process documentation, blog [music] content, or structured markdown files inside build systems. The tool runs locally and fits into JavaScript-based development workflows where Markdown acts as a primary content format. This helps developers turn markdown content into structured data for web applications. Explore the repository to process Markdown programmatically. Project number 13, Playright for Swift. Swift bindings for Playright browser automation. Playright for Swift is an open-source library that brings Playright browser automation capabilities to Swift applications. It provides Swift interfaces that allow developers to control browsers, run automated tests, and interact with web pages using Playright features. The library communicates with the Playright runtime and exposes functions to launch browsers, navigate pages, and execute actions in automated workflows. Developers integrated into Swift projects to run browser automation tasks directly from Swift Code. This helps teams write automated web tests and automation scripts in the Swift ecosystem. Explore the repository to automate browsers with Swift. Project number 14, Font Trio. Tool for managing and previewing font combinations. Font Trio is an open- source tool that helps designers and developers experiment with combinations of fonts. It provides an environment where different font pairings can be previewed and evaluated together. The system loads font files and renders sample text so users can see how multiple fonts work in the same layout. Developers and designers use it to explore typography choices when building user interfaces or branding assets. The tool runs locally and focuses on simplifying typography experimentation. Explore the repository to test font combinations in your design workflow. Project number 15, Spectre. Configuration setup for the Ghosty Terminal. Spectre is an open-source configuration repository designed for the Ghosty Terminal environment. It provides configuration files and setup instructions that customize the appearance and behavior of the Ghosty terminal. The repository includes settings for themes, layout, and terminal preferences that users can apply to their own Ghosty installation. Developers clone or copy the configuration to personalize their terminal environment quickly. This helps users create a consistent and optimized terminal setup. Explore the repository to customize your Ghosty terminal configuration. Project number 16, Planning with Files. File-based system for structured project planning. Planning with files is an open-source system that organizes project planning using plain files and directory structures. It defines a workflow where tasks, goals, and planning documents exist as files that can be tracked with standard file tools and version control systems. Developers and teams store plans in text files and manage progress through edits and updates inside the repository. The approach keeps planning transparent and easy to track alongside code. This helps teams manage planning without specialized project management software. Explore the repository to organize planning through files. Project number 17, Deeptutor, AI framework for personalized tutoring systems. Deeptutor is an open-source framework designed to build AI tutoring systems powered by large language models. It provides components that manage student interaction, knowledge tracking, and instructional reasoning. During tutoring sessions, developers configure learning tasks, prompts, and evaluation logic while the system coordinates conversations between students and AI tutors. The framework manages context and learning feedback, so tutoring interactions adapt to the learner's needs. This helps researchers and developers build educational AI systems. Explore the repository to experiment with AIdriven tutoring workflows. Project number 18, GitHub Copilot CLI SDKs. SDKs for building extensions to Copilot CLI. GitHub Copilot CLI SDKs is an open-source collection of software development kits that help developers build extensions for the GitHub Copilot CLI. The project provides libraries and examples that allow tools to integrate with the C-Pilot command line workflow. Developers create plugins that connect external services or commands to the C-pilot CLI environment. The SDKs handle communication between extensions and the C-pilot CLI runtime so new capabilities can be added. This helps developers extend how Copilot assists inside the terminal. Explore the repository to build a C-pilot CLI extension. Project number 19, Zerbrew, lightweight package manager for local development tools. Zerbrew is an open-source package manager designed to install and manage development tools without relying on systemwide package managers. It downloads and installs tools into a local environment so projects can manage their own tool chain dependencies. Developers use simple commands to fetch and run tools required by a project without affecting the global system setup. The tool focuses on reproducible environments and lightweight setup. This helps teams manage development utilities within project directories. Explore the repository to manage tools with a local package manager. Project number 20, Hive, framework for building collaborative AI agents. Hive is an open-source framework designed to coordinate multiple AI agents working together on tasks. It provides structures that allow agents to communicate, share context, and collaborate while solving problems. Developers define agents, tasks, and communication flows. Then the framework manages how agents interact during execution. The system integrates with language models and tool interfaces so agents can reason and perform actions in a coordinated workflow. This helps developers experiment with collaborative agent architectures. Explore the repository to build multi- aent AI systems. Thanks for watching. See you in the next update.

transcript: Every week, developers release new tools and frameworks as open-source GitHub projects. In this weekly update, we look at top trending open-source GitHub projects this week, so you can quickly see what developers are building. These projects focus on useful developer tools, AI workflows, automation, and infrastructure. Without wasting time, let's get started. >> Before we jump into today's project updates, here's a quick announcement for everyone. We've launched a brand new YouTube channel called AI Agent Studio dedicated entirely to AI Agent projects, tutorials, and tools. So, if you're interested in staying up to date with the latest AI Agent open source projects, learning how to build your own agents, or exploring cuttingedge agent frameworks, make sure to check it out. Subscribe now to get weekly videos, in-depth guides, and real time project breakdowns. The link is right there in the description. Don't miss it. All right, let's get into today's video. >> Project number one, BitNet CPP, efficient inference engine for one bit language models. Bitnet CPP is an open-source C++ inference framework from Microsoft designed to run one-bit large language models such as BitNet B1.58 efficiently on standard hardware. The project provides optimized compute kernels that execute model inference using extremely low precision weights, enabling fast and lossless execution of these models. Developers compile the framework locally and load supported bitnet model weights to run inference directly on CPUs and GPUs with future support for additional accelerators. This approach reduces compute and memory requirements, making local LLM deployment practical for developers building efficient AI systems. Explore the repository to run a one-bit LLM locally. Project number two, Open Rag end platform for building rag applications. Open Rag is an open-source retrieval augmented generation platform designed to build AI systems that search documents and generate contextual responses. It runs as a single package built on Langflow for workflow orchestration, Dockling for document processing, and open search for indexing and retrieval. Developers ingest documents, create retrieval pipelines, and connect language models to answer questions using stored knowledge. The platform handles document parsing, embedding, indexing, and query workflows inside one environment. This helps developers build document aware assistance and knowledge tools faster. Explore the repository to start building rag pipelines. Project number three, Light Panda browser. Lightweight headless browser built for automation. Light Panda browser is an open-source headless browser designed for fast web automation and AI agent workflows. It runs without a graphical interface and executes JavaScript, parses HTML, and exposes browser control through the Chrome DevTools protocol, so tools like Puppeteer or Playright can connect to it. The browser loads pages, executes scripts, and returns DOM data for scraping, testing, or training pipelines. It runs locally through a binary or Docker container and focuses on automation rather than rendering. This helps developers run largecale web tasks efficiently. Try connecting your automation scripts to it. Project number four, Prompt FU evaluation and red team testing for LLM apps. Prompt FFU is an open-source CLI and library that helps developers test and evaluate large language model applications. It runs automated prompt tests, compares outputs across models, and generates evaluation results through configuration files and command line commands. Developers define prompts, inputs, and expected behaviors, then run evaluations that call supported model APIs and collect outputs for analysis. The tool also supports security testing through red team style checks for vulnerabilities in AI systems. It integrates with development workflows and CI environments so teams can track model quality and safety. Run evaluations locally to validate your LLM workflows. Project number five, DOL SQL database with git style version control. Dolt is an open-source SQL database that adds gitst style version control to structured data. It behaves like a relational database with tables and queries while allowing developers to commit, branch, merge, and diff database changes using familiar git concepts. The system stores data in a format that tracks changes over time so teams can inspect history, collaborate on data sets, and manage schema updates. Developers interact with it through SQL commands or a command line interface that mirrors Git workflows. This helps teams treat data changes like code. Try versioning your database with it. Project number six, A2UI system for generating interfaces from AI actions. A2UI is an open-source framework that converts AI actions into structured user interfaces. It provides a runtime where an AI system outputs structured descriptions of UI components and actions and the framework renders them as interactive interfaces. Developers define the component catalog and behaviors that the AI can use. Then the runtime interprets the AI output and builds the interface dynamically. The system connects AI reasoning with controlled UI generation so applications can present model outputs as real interfaces instead of plain text. Explore the project to experiment with AIdriven UI generation. Project number seven, Project ARRI AI assistant framework for interactive agents. Project ARRI is an open-source framework for building interactive AI assistants that combine language models with tools in memory. It provides components for managing conversations, agent behaviors, and integrations with external services. Developers run the system locally and configure models, tools, and data sources so the assistant can reason, perform tasks, and respond in structured conversations. The framework coordinates prompts, context management and tool execution to support agent workflows. This helps developers prototype and run customizable AI companions or assistance. Explore the repository to start building an AI agent. Project number eight, Arabos framework for self-improving AI agent loops. Araboros is an open-source framework that experiments with recursive AI agent workflows where agents generate tasks, execute them, and feed results back into the system. It structures agent execution as iterative loops that produce new instructions, evaluate results, and update context for the next cycle. Developers run the system locally and connect it to language model APIs to create autonomous reasoning workflows. The framework manages prompts, task memory, and execution flow to coordinate agent behavior. This approach helps developers explore continuous agent improvement. Experiment with it to build iterative AI systems. Project number nine, CLI anything, natural language interface for command line tasks. CLI anything is an open-source AI tool that turns natural language instructions into executable command line operations. It connects large language models with a CLI environment so users can describe tasks in plain language and receive generated commands that perform the requested action. The system interprets user intent, maps it to shell operations, and executes commands within the terminal environment. Developers can use it to simplify complex command workflows and automate routine operations. This helps teams interact with development tools through conversational instructions. Try describing a task and let the CLI generate the command. Project number 10, Astro MD4X Markdown extension system for Astro content. Astro MD4X is an open-source plug-in that extends markdown capabilities in Astro projects. It integrates with the Astro framework to add advanced markdown processing features that support richer content structures. Developers write markdown files and the plug-in processes them through the Astro build pipeline, enabling custom components, syntax extensions, and enhanced rendering inside Astro pages. The system works within Astro's content workflow, so Markdown can power dynamic pages and documentation sites. This helps developers keep content simple while still building structured web pages. Try adding it to an Astro project to extend markdown rendering. Project number 11, Awesome AI Agents curated collection of open-source AI agent projects. Awesome AI Agents is a community-maintained repository that collects and organizes open-source AI agent frameworks, tools, and research projects in one place. It acts as a curated reference list where developers explore different agent architectures, frameworks, and supporting tools used to build autonomous AI systems. The repository groups projects by categories such as frameworks, multi-agent systems, research projects, and practical applications. Developers use it as a discovery hub to compare approaches and identify tools for building agents powered by large language models. Browse the repository to explore the growing ecosystem of AI agents. Project number 12, Defuts clean content from web pages. Defut is an open-source commandline tool that extracts the readable content from web pages and removes clutter such as ads, navigation, and layout elements. It takes a web page URL or HTML input and processes the page to isolate the main article text and structure. The tool runs locally and outputs simplified content that is easier to read or process in scripts and AI workflows. Developers use it when preparing web content for analysis, archiving, or language model input. Try it to quickly turn messy web pages into clean text. Project number 13, Open Utter. Open framework for building conversational voice assistance. Open Utter is an open-source framework for building voice-driven conversational assistance. It connects speech recognition, language models, and texttospech components to create interactive voice experiences. Developers configure pipelines that capture spoken input, convert it to text, process the request with AI models, and generate spoken responses. The system supports modular integrations, so different speech engines and models can be connected depending on the environment. It runs locally and focuses on providing a flexible architecture for voice-based AI applications. Explore the project to start building a custom voice assistant. Project number 14, Athys. Lightweight framework for building AI agents. Athys is an open-source framework that helps developers build AI agents capable of reasoning and interacting with tools. It provides a structured environment where developers define prompts, tools, and execution flows while language models generate decisions and actions. The framework manages conversation context, tool invocation, and agent reasoning loops to support autonomous workflows. Developers run the system locally and connect it to language model APIs to power the agents reasoning. This helps teams prototype agent-based applications more easily. Explore the repository to start building your own agent workflows. Project number 15, Auto Research AI system that conducts automated research workflows. Auto research is an open-source project that explores automated research processes driven by language models. The system coordinates prompts, search steps, and reasoning loops so an AI agent can gather information, analyze sources, and produce structured outputs. It connects to language model APIs and external tools to retrieve information and iterate on research tasks. Developers run the workflow locally and modify prompts or tools to experiment with different research strategies. This project shows how AI agents can assist with knowledge discovery tasks. Explore the repository to experiment with automated research pipelines. Project number 16, Post-Train bench benchmark suite for evaluating post-training methods. Post-train bench is an open-source benchmark designed to evaluate post-training techniques used to improve large language models. It provides data sets, evaluation tasks, and measurement workflows that help researchers test methods such as alignment tuning, instruction tuning, and optimization. And after base model training, developers run evaluation pipelines that apply post-training strategies and measure their performance across defined tasks. The benchmark helps researchers compare different techniques in a consistent environment. This makes it easier to study how post-training changes model behavior. Explore the repository to test post-training approaches. Project number 17, Koolifi, self-hosted platform for deploying applications and databases. Koolifi is an open-source platform that allows developers to deploy applications, databases, and services on their own infrastructure. It provides a web interface that connects to servers and manages deployments using containers and Git repositories. Developers push code to a repository and the platform builds, deploys, and manages the application automatically on connected servers. It supports multiple runtimes and databases while keeping control fully self-hosted. This helps teams run production services without relying on external cloud platforms. Explore the repository to run your own self-hosted deployment platform. Project number 18, Okash universal caching library for JavaScript environments. Okache is an open-source caching library designed for modern JavaScript and server environments. It provides a simple interface to store and retrieve cached values while supporting different storage backends. Developers integrate it into Node.js JS, serverless or edge applications to cache API responses, computed values or application data. The library manages expiration, serialization and storage adapters so the same caching logic works across environments. This helps developers improve performance and reduce repeated computation in distributed applications. Explore the repository to add flexible caching to your projects. Project number 19, VarLock tool for managing environment variables securely. Varlock is an open-source tool that helps developers manage environment variables safely across development and deployment environments. It stores configuration variables in encrypted files and provides commands to lock or unlock them when needed. Developers keep sensitive values such as API keys in version controlled repositories without exposing the actual secrets. The tool integrates with local development workflows and deployment pipelines so applications can access variables securely at runtime. This helps teams handle configuration and secrets more safely. Explore the repository to secure environment variables in your projects. Project number 20, Markdown View. Native Markdown rendering component for Apple platforms. Markdown View is an open-source Swift component that renders Markdown content in applications built for Apple platforms. It integrates with Swift UI and UIKit so developers can display formatted markdown text directly inside their apps. The component parses markdown syntax and converts it into native UI elements while supporting styling and layout customization. Developers add it to their iOS or Mac OS projects to render documentation, notes, or dynamic content without building a custom renderer. This simplifies the process of displaying markdown in Apple applications. Try integrating it into your app to render Markdown content. Thanks for watching. See you in the next update.

transcript: Most AI notebook tools are still basically smart folders. You upload some docs, ask a few questions, maybe generate a podcast, and that's where it ends. The moment you need live email, today's calendar, active Jira tickets, and a teammate in the same thread, the whole thing starts feeling small. This is where Surf Sense got really interesting because the real problem usually is not summarizing one static PDF. It's that the context you actually need is spread across Gmail, Drive, Notion, GitHub, Jira, Linear, your calendar, random web pages, and whatever somebody dropped into chat 10 minutes ago. So, every morning starts the same way. 20 tabs open, three different summaries, copy pasting context into an LLM, and still not being fully sure you're looking at the latest version of anything. SurfSense feels like someone took the core idea behind notebook LM, removed the training wheels, and turned it into a live knowledge workspace. You create a search space, connect the tools you already use, choose the model you want behind the scenes, and then chat with that whole knowledge base in one place, not just your files, your workflows. All right. From the main dashboard, the layout is pretty easy to understand. On the left, you have your search space, your chats, inbox, and documents. In the center, you have the main chat canvas. At the top, you can switch models. Then, inside the documents view, you can either upload files or open the connectors panel and start wiring in live sources. So, even though there's a lot going on under the hood, the interface does a good job of making it feel manageable, let me show you the most useful use case first, because this is the one that instantly made sense to me. Imagine it's early morning and instead of manually checking your inbox, your calendar, and your task manager, you connect Gmail, Google calendar, and Jira or linear into the same search space. You let those sources sync and then you ask SurfSense for one clean daily briefing. Not a generic summary, a useful one. Something like, "Give me the urgent emails. Show me the meetings that actually matter today. Pull the tickets that are blocked or due soon. Tell me what's likely to create context switching and then turn the whole thing into a short audio podcast I can listen to on the commute. That is a fundamentally different category of tool. You're no longer just chatting with documents you uploaded last week. You're building a live assistant around the systems you already run your day in. And because SurfSense supports scheduled reindexing on connectors with frequencies that go as low as every five minutes, the assistant can stay meaningfully current instead of becoming a frozen snapshot of last night's state. That freshness matters more than people think. If your calendar changed 20 minutes ago or somebody added a new ticket or an email thread suddenly became urgent, a static notebook is already behind. SurfSense is trying to solve that exact gap between research assistant and operational assistant. And the podcast angle is actually smart. A lot of AI products add audio because it sounds cool in a launch tweet. Here it fits the workflow. You already have this pile of low-level context you need to process anyway. Turning it into a short audio overview makes sense because you can absorb it while you're walking, driving, or just getting ready. Instead of reading six fragmented updates, you listen once and move on. Now, here is the second big reason this stands out. Research scale. Notebook LM is great at giving you a neat space around a set of sources, but Google's own help docs still describe the free tier in terms of limits like 50 sources per notebook. SurfSense feels more like a true workspace. In the cloud plans, the pricing is framed around pages of content instead of tiny notebook style source buckets. And in the self-hosted docs, the page limit setting is unlimited by default. So the mental model changes. You stop asking, "How do I squeeze this project into one notebook?" And you start asking, "What should live inside this search space?" That shift matters a lot. If you're doing serious research, agency work, product work, or team knowledge management. You can connect docs, repos, notes, tickets, web pages, calendars, and then query across all of it as one environment. That feels much closer to a company brain than a smart folder. The other part I like is that SurfSense is not trying to force one model world view on you. The product site and readme both lean hard into model flexibility. You can use a long list of hosted models. And for self-hosted setups, there are local options like VLM, Alma, Llama, CPP, and LM Studio. So, if you care about latency, cost, privacy, or just model preference, you have real choices. And when you do start asking questions, the point is not just to get a polished answer back. The point is to get cited answers grounded in your actual knowledge base. Now, this is the part that matters for teams. The docs show a real-time collaboration flow built around shared search spaces. You invite teammates, they join the space, you toggle chats to shared, and then everyone can work inside the same thread in real time. Messages appear live for participants. And you can add comments directly on messages and tag people for feedback or follow-up. That sounds simple, but it solves a very common problem. Most AI research tools still break the moment an answer needs another person in the loop. The workflow becomes copy the answer, paste it into Slack, explain what the AI did, ask for feedback, lose the original context, and then repeat. Here, the discussion happens next to the answer inside the workspace itself. I also think the self-hosted and open- source path deserves real attention, especially for this audience. The cloud version is obviously the easiest place to start, but SurfSense also has a self-hosted build, and the docs include a oneline Docker install for Linux and Mac OS, plus a PowerShell version for Windows. So for privacy focused users or people who just want control over the stack, there is a credible path to running this on your own infrastructure. And it's not just self-hosted in the vague marketing sense. The Docker docs expose configuration for local document parsing, local texttospech, local speechtoext, local model endpoints, connector credentials, real-time sync services, and more. So, if your ideal setup is closer to bring your own models, bring your own environment. Keep the system under your control. Surf Sense is clearly designed with that mindset in mind. Now, honest take. If all you want is the easiest possible way to throw a few PDFs into an AI notebook and chat with them, SurfSense might be more tool than you need, Notebook LM is still simpler. That is part of its appeal. SurfSense starts to make real sense when your knowledge is spread across tools, when you want fresher context, when you need model flexibility, or when multiple people need to work inside the same AI environment. So, this is not just a better PDF chatbot. It's trying to become a knowledge operating layer. That's a bigger promise and it also means a bit more setup. But in this case, the setup is the point. So who is this for? I think SurfSense makes the most sense for founders, operators, researchers, PMs, agencies, dev teams, and honestly anybody whose day is already fragmented across too many systems. Start with one practical search space. Connect the sources you already check every day. Try the morning commute workflow first, Gmail, Calendar, Jira or Linear. Then generate a short podcast briefing. After that, add documents. Add a shared chat and see whether the experience starts replacing tab hopping for real. Because that is the test, not whether the homepage sounds impressive, not whether the demo looks polished, whether this actually helps you think and act faster inside your own work. And SurfSense, at least from what I've seen here, is one of the first tools in this category that feels like it understands that

transcript: Every week, developers release new open-source tools that help improve how we build, secure, and manage software. This weekly update highlights top trending open-source and best dev tool projects this week, so you can quickly discover useful tools without spending hours searching. From AI agent frameworks to security scanners and developer workflow tools, these projects are gaining attention in the open-source community. Without wasting time, let's get started. Before we jump into today's project updates, here's a quick announcement for everyone, we've launched a brand new YouTube channel called AI Agent Studio, dedicated entirely to AI Agent projects, tutorials, and tools. So, if you're interested in staying up to date with the latest AI agent open source projects, learning how to build your own agents, or exploring cuttingedge agent frameworks, make sure to check it out. Subscribe now to get weekly videos, in-depth guides, and realtime project breakdowns. The link is right there in the description. Don't miss it. All right, let's get into today's video. Project number one, Shannon autonomous AI pentester that executes real exploits. Shannon is an open-source autonomous AI pentesting tool designed to find and prove security vulnerabilities in web applications. It analyzes a target application source code and running environment, maps the attack surface, and then attempts real exploits using its own browser and command line tools. Instead of only flagging potential risks, the system combines codeware analysis with live testing and integrate security tools like end mapap, subfinder, what web, and schema thesis to explore attack paths and validate weaknesses. It generates detailed reports with reproducible proof ofconcept exploits so developers and security teams can fix confirmed issues quickly. Try running Shannon against your web app to uncover real vulnerabilities. Project number two, Trivy, unified security scanner for modern development environments. Trivy is an open-source security scanning tool that helps developers detect vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and secrets across software artifacts. It works as a CLI tool that scans container images, file stems, git repositories, virtual machine images, and Kubernetes environments to identify security issues before deployment. The scanner analyzes dependencies, configuration files, and package metadata to surface risks directly in development workflows. It integrates easily into CI pipelines and developer tooling, so security checks run continuously alongside builds. This helps dev sec ops teams catch real security problems early. Run Trievy in your pipeline and keep your builds secure. Project number three, MCP for beginners. Practical curriculum for building model context protocol apps. MCP for beginners is an open-source learning repository that teaches developers how to build applications using the model context protocol. It acts as a structured curriculum with practical examples that demonstrate how AI systems communicate with external tools and services through MCP servers. The repository includes cross- language examples in environments like NE, Java, TypeScript, JavaScript, Rust, and Python, showing how to configure sessions, expose tools, and orchestrate services. Developers learn how to connect AI models to real systems through modular workflows and service interfaces. Explore the lessons and start building MCP-based AI integrations. Project number four, CodeBuff, terminal AI agent that edits entire code bases. CodeBuff is an open-source AI coding assistant that runs as a CLI tool and edits code bases through natural language instructions. It coordinates multiple specialized agents that analyze the repository structure, plan changes, update files, and review results across the project. When developers request a change, agents scan the codebase to locate relevant files, generate a plan for modifications, apply edits, and validate the updates so the project remains consistent. It runs locally in the terminal and can execute commands, update configurations, and modify code across frameworks and languages. Developers can automate large code changes quickly. Try running code buff inside your project repository. Project number five, open sandbox. Secure sandbox runtime for autonomous AI agents. Open Sandbox is an open-source sandbox platform designed to run AI agents in isolated execution environments. It provides unified sandbox APIs and multi- language SDKs so developers can create controlled environments where agents safely execute code, access field stems, or run tools. The platform supports runtimes built on Docker and Kubernetes, allowing both local development sandboxes and distributed clusters. It includes built-in environments for command execution, code interpreters, browser automation, and desktop interfaces. This helps teams safely run coding agents, evaluation systems, and training workflows without exposing the host machine. Deploy open sandbox and give your AI agents a secure execution environment. Project number six, Learn Claude Code, hands-on guide for building Claude coding agents. Learn Claude Code is an open-source learning repository that teaches developers how to build coding workflows using cloud-based tools. It provides practical examples that demonstrate how developers structure prompts, manage code tasks, and connect cloud coding assistance to real projects. The repository focuses on practical development workflows where AI reads repositories, edits files, and executes coding tasks through structured prompts and tooling. Developers can study the examples to understand how cloud-driven coding systems interact with project files and automation tools. This helps engineers build reliable AI coding workflows. Explore the examples and experiment with cloud coding setups. Project number seven, Supererset, web analytics platform for interactive data exploration. Superset is an open-source data exploration and visualization platform designed for analysts and data teams. It runs as a web application that connects to many SQLbased databases, and lets users explore data sets, write queries, and build interactive dashboards. The platform includes a SQL editor, visualization tools, and dashboard components that render charts directly from database queries. Data stays in connected databases while Superset handles query execution and visualization in the browser. This allows teams to analyze large data sets without moving them into a separate analytic system. Deploy Supererset and start exploring your data through interactive dashboards. Project number eight, Claude Skills reusable capability modules for Claude agents. Claude Skills is an open-source collection of reusable capability modules designed for cloud-based AI agents. It provides structured skills that define how an AI agent performs specific tasks such as interacting with tools, processing data, or executing workflows. Developers can integrate these skills into claw-driven systems to extend agent behavior without rewriting core logic. Each skill acts as a modular capability that an agent can invoke when solving a task. This approach helps developers build more structured and maintainable AI agents, explore the skills, and start composing new agent capabilities. Project number nine, Symphfony framework for orchestrating multi-agent AI workflows. Symfony is an open-source framework for building and orchestrating multi-agent AI systems. It provides a structured environment where multiple AI agents collaborate on complex tasks through defined roles and communication patterns. Developers can design workflows where agents exchange messages, process intermediate results, and coordinate actions to solve larger problems. The framework handles agent coordination and task orchestration while developers define the logic for each agent. This helps teams experiment with collaborative AI architectures in real applications. Build your first coordinated agent workflow with Symphony. Project number 10, Claude Code best practice. Practical patterns for reliable cloud coding workflows. Claude Code Best Practice is an open-source repository that documents recommended patterns for working with cloud-based coding tools. It focuses on practical guidance for structuring prompts, organizing repositories, and managing AI assisted development tasks. The examples demonstrate how developers guide Claude to read repositories, modify files, and produce consistent code changes during automated workflows. By following these patterns, teams can reduce errors and maintain predictable behavior when using AI coding assistance. Study the examples and apply these practices to improve your clawed coding workflow. Project number 11. Vier Ames agent. Persistent autonomous AI agent that learns over time. Vier agent is an open-source autonomous AI agent designed to run continuously on a developer machine or server and assist with real tasks across environments. It operates as a persistent agent that connects to messaging platforms like Telegram, Slack, Discord, and terminal interfaces through a single gateway process. The system stores long-term memory by writing reusable skill documents that record how it solves tasks, allowing it to recall workflows later instead of starting from scratch. It also runs commands through terminal backends such as local shells, Docker, or SSH environments, and can automate scheduled jobs or spawn sub aents for parallel work. This helps developers run an AI assistant that remembers projects and executes tasks directly in real environments. Try installing Hermes agent and run a persistent AI operator on your server. Project number 12. Divekit realtime platform for audio, video, and data. Livekit is an open-source real-time communication platform that helps developers build applications with live audio, video, and data streaming. It provides server infrastructure and SDKs that manage WebRTC connections between clients while handling media routing, participant management, and network reliability. Developers integrate LiveKit with web, mobile, or back-end applications to enable real-time collaboration, streaming or voice interactions. The platform supports self-hosting or cloud deployment, so teams control how media traffic is processed and delivered. This helps developers build scalable interactive applications, deploy live kit, and power real-time experiences in your app. Project number 13, Web Haptics, browser library for programmable haptic feedback. Web haptics is an open-source JavaScript library that enables developers to create programmable haptic feedback directly in web applications. It provides APIs that trigger vibration patterns and tactile responses through supported devices using browser capabilities. Developers define custom vibration sequences that respond to user interactions such as button presses, notifications, or game events. The library abstracts device vibration controls so haptic effects can be integrated easily in web interfaces. This helps improve accessibility and interaction feedback in browser experiences. Add web haptics to your project and experiment with tactile web interactions. Project number 14, CMU connection multiplexer for shared network ports. CMUX is an open-source networking library that allows multiple protocols to share a single TCP port. It works by inspecting the first bytes of an incoming connection and routing the request to the correct server handler based on protocol patterns. Developers use Cmox to run services such as HTTP, gRPC, and other custom protocols on the same port without separate listeners. The library integrates directly into Go applications and manages connection routing internally while maintaining normal server behavior. transcript: Every developer in 2026 has the same problem. You open your editor, you write one line of code, and suddenly a dozen different AI agents are arguing in your terminal about how to do it better. And if you're one of those weirdos like me who actually enjoys the craft of writing code, congratulations. You're officially living in the dark ages of slop overflow. Instead of grinding for hours and earning those sweet dopamine hits line by line, you now just tell the AI what you want and watch it hallucinate an entire codebase. Writing code isn't fun anymore. Layoffs are intensifying. And even the CEO of Replet said that nowadays knowing how to code is actually a disadvantage. >> Not having a coding experience is becoming an advantage. >> Building a product is more efficient than ever. Unless you're a stupid programmer who cares about stupid things like architecture and security. >> Coders get lost in the details. >> But he's absolutely right. The hard truth is that we're not going back to the good old days of handcrafted code. And the only way forward is to embrace the chaos and learn how to enslave the machines. In today's video, we'll look at seven different open- source projects you've never heard of that will help you whip your AI agents into shape and build highly effective slot pipelines. It is March 12th, 2026 and you're watching the code report. In the past, if you were an indie full stack developer, it meant you had to have skills on the front end, backend. You had to understand DevOps, security, UI, UX design, and a bunch of other BS. But nowadays, you don't need to learn all that stuff. You just need to hire the right agent. And a tool that can help you do that quickly is the agency, which is a free and open- source project that provides agent templates for basically every job role you would find at a startup, like a front-end developer, back-end developer, security engineer, a growth hacker, Twitter engager, and many others. You can easily combine all these agents together in claude code, which can more efficiently help you go from zero to an actual product without needing to directly implement every personality and skill. That's cool, but when you put these agents to work, how do you know your prompts are any good? Well, that's where Prompt Fu comes in. Another open- source tool that was just recently acquired by OpenAI that you can think of like a unit testing framework for your prompts. If you're using AI to build an app that lets the end user interact with AI, also half the battle is figuring out if you're using the best model with the best prompt. But prompt FU lets you test different prompts with different models to optimize what's going to actually work best in your application. On top of that, it can also do automated red team attacks to find out if your app is vulnerable to things like prompt injection, which is important because if your chatbot can be tricked into revealing your API keys by a 14-year-old on Discord, your app is probably going to fail. The failing sucks, but it's a lot easier to not fail when you can predict the future. And Mirrorish can help you do that. It's a multi- aent AI prediction engine that starts by extracting a bunch of data from the internet, like breaking news and financial trends. It then uses that data to create a digital world where multiple agents with independent personalities then react to and discuss the data almost like a miniature evolving artificial social network. Yeah, it might be in Chinese, but if you don't know how to speak Chinese yet, all I can say to you is low hola, you're falling behind. Like for example, if you want an app idea that's guaranteed to make you a billion dollars, you can spin up Micro Fish to analyze trends at the macro and micro level, then predict a strategy that's guaranteed to make you rich. It's really that easy. But here's the problem. You go to build that app and the UI has these dumb purple gradients like every single other vibe coded app. Well, to fix that, you need Impeccable, an open- source project optimized for front-end design. It's a skill that comes with 17 different commands that can help your UI not suck so much. Like, one thing that drives me crazy is that many AI chatbots create UIs that are way too complex. Well, with impeccable, we can use the distill command to simplify everything in one go. Then we can use commands like colorize to add our brand colors. Then slowly add in commands like animate and delight to make the UI look more unique and special. But perhaps the single most important skill of the modern Vibe engineer is managing context. If the context is garbage, the output is garbage. An open- source project trying to make your context better is Open Viking, a database designed specifically for AI agents. Instead of jamming everything into a vector database, Open Viking organizes an agents memory, resources, and skills into the file system. Not only is that a sane way to unify your context, but it also uses a tiered loading system which can dramatically reduce token consumption and save you a bunch of money. And it automatically compresses content and refineses long-term memory, which will make your agent smarter the more you use it. But depending on your project, you may not need an agent that's more smarter. You may need an agent that's more based. And that's where Heretic comes in. Virtually all models out there have guardrails that prevent you from doing fun things like cooking method in your shed or building high yield thermonuclear warheads. Heretic allows you to remove this draconian woke censorship using a technique called obliteration. This approach allows the tool to be completely automatic and doesn't require any expensive post training. All you have to do is take a smart yet highly censored model like Google's Gemma, run this tool from the command line, and now you have a model without the bubble wrap that will obey any command. But maybe that's not even enough to satisfy your unhinged ambitions. In that case, you may want to just build your own LLM from scratch. And believe it or not, you can actually do that with Nano Chat, which implements the entire LLM pipeline, including tokenization, pre-training, fine-tuning for chat, evaluation, and a web UI, so you can actually talk to it. What's crazy though is that you can use it to train your own small language model for about $100 in GPU time. It's not going to be Claw, GPT5, or Gemini, but at least it gives you a model that you have absolute control over. But the only thing that's a bigger waste of time than writing code by hand is going to meetings. And that's why you need to know about Recall AI, the sponsor of today's video. A hugging face space that showcases a next generation AI system designed to truly understand documents, not just read text. This interactive demo lets you upload an image or provide an image URL, choose what to recognize, such as full document layout, plain text, tables, formulas, charts, or seals, and instantly see the structured results extracted from the page. Under the hood, the app runs with a Graddio interface and connects to the Paddle OCR VL1.5 model, a compact 0.9B parameter vision language model that combines a Navit style dynamic resolution visual encoder with the Ernie 4.50.3B language model to interpret complex document elements across many languages. The model achieves state-of-the-art results, reaching about 94.5% accuracy on the OmniDoc Bench V1.5 benchmark while staying efficient enough for practical deployment. Developers, researchers, and businesses can experiment with the workflow. Try the demo. Upload a document and watch AI transform complex pages into usable information in seconds. Project number two, zero. Omni multimodal AI that generates vector animations. Let's explore Omni, an experimental hugging face space that turns simple prompts into fully structured Lahi animations. you can instantly preview and download. Instead of producing pixel videos like most generative models, this demo focuses on vector animation, meaning the output is lightweight JSON that can power icons, UI animations, or web graphics at any resolution. The interface works like a playground. You can describe an animation, upload an image reference, or even provide short visual cues. Then the system generates a Lahi animation file and displays it live in the browser. Behind the scenes, Omniotti is built on a multimodal vision language architecture extended with a specialized tokenizer that converts Lahi JSON structures into model friendly tokens allowing AI to generate shapes, layers, and motion parameters directly. The project is trained on a large multimodal data set called MMA DH2M containing millions of annotated animations enabling the model to follow complex instructions about motion, objects, and style. For designers, developers, and product teams, this matters because micro animations are everywhere, from mobile apps to dashboards, but usually require manual design tools. With Omni, a developer could simply prompt a bouncing notification bell and instantly get a usable animation file ready for a website or app. Project number three, Tracker Playground, interactive computer vision tracking sandbox. Meet Tracker Playground, a hugging face space built by Rooflow that lets you experiment with modern object tracking pipelines directly in your browser. The demo works like a visual lab for video AI. Upload a short clip, choose a detection model and tracking algorithm, tweak settings such as confidence thresholds or classes, and then watch the system follow objects frame by frame across the video. Under the hood, the interface combines computer vision detection models with multiobject tracking methods, letting users visualize how bounding boxes move and persist across frames. This mirrors how production systems track vehicles, people, or products in surveillance, robotics, and retail analytics. Robooflow, the organization behind the space, focuses on tools that help engineers build and deploy computer vision models, and its ecosystem already supports thousands of models, and large annotated data sets used in real world applications. The value of this playground is that it removes the complexity of setting up pipelines locally. Instead of wiring together detection models, trackers, and visualization code, you simply upload a video and test different combinations interactively. A practical use case could be evaluating how well a tracker follows multiple players in a sports clip or monitoring vehicles in a traffic camera feed. Project number four, Bit Dance, large-scale generative choreography AI playground. Introducing Bit Dance, a hugging face space that demonstrates a large generative model designed to create expressive dance motion from AI prompts. The demo provides a simple interface where users can experiment with a 14 billion parameter dance generation model that outputs structured movement patterns at a 64- frame resolution setting, allowing users to preview how choreography evolves over time. Instead of generating text or static images, the system focuses on motion generation, a growing area of multimodal AI that blends language understanding with temporal modeling of movement. The workflow is designed as a prompt to motion pipeline. Enter a description of a dance style or mood, run inference, and the system produces visualized motion results representing how a digital dancer might move. Under the hood, the project integrates large generative model techniques similar to diffusion or transformer-based motion models used in research for animation and virtual characters. These tools are increasingly important as AI moves into creative domains like gaming, digital performers, and virtual production. For creators and researchers, the space acts as a rapid prototyping environment to explore AIdriven choreography without installing heavy frameworks. A practical example might be generating dance moves for a virtual avatar in a game or testing choreography ideas for an animated music video. Project number five, Kitten TTS demo. Tiny AI that speaks natural text. Meet Kitten TTS demo. A lightweight textto-spech playground that shows how small models can still produce natural sounding voices. In this hugging face space, the workflow is refreshingly simple. Type a piece of text, choose a voice style, select a model optimized either for speed or quality, and the system instantly generates a playable speech clip. The demo runs the Kitten TTS family of models, including ultra compact variants such as Kitten TS Nano with around 15 million parameters designed for highquality speech synthesis while remaining small enough to run on everyday hardware. Unlike massive cloudonly voice models, Kitten TS focuses on efficiency, keeping the model under about 25 megaps, so it can run without GPU acceleration and even work on edge devices or laptops. That makes it especially useful for developers building privacy-friendly assistants, offline apps, or embedded voice interfaces. For example, a developer building a smart home dashboard could integrate kitten TS locally to generate spoken alerts without sending audio requests to the cloud. The space demonstrates how modern speech synthesis is becoming faster, smaller, and easier to deploy. Project number six, Voxrol subtitles, AI that turns audio into smart captions. Let's explore Voxrol subtitles, a hugging face space that transforms raw audio or video into accurate subtitles with timing, speaker detection, and optional translation. The interface is straightforward. Upload a media file, run the transcription pipeline, and the system returns captions with word level timestamps and structured subtitle output ready for editing or export. The demo is powered by the Voxrol speech models developed by Mistral AI, a family of open audio language models designed to understand both speech and text with strong multilingual performance. Voxrol supports long- form context windows of up to 32K tokens, enabling it to process audio sessions that last tens of minutes while still answering questions or summarizing the content. This matters because many modern AI workflows, podcasts, meetings, lectures, and video production need more than basic transcription. They need structured understanding of what was said and when. With Voxrol subtitles, creators or developers can quickly turn a recorded webinar into captioned video, searchable transcripts, or multilingual subtitles, making content easier to publish, analyze, and reuse. Project number seven, Lux TTS Realtime AI voice cloning playground. Introducing Lux TTS voice cloning, a hugging face that lets you recreate a person's voice and make it speak any text you type. The demo works through a simple two-step workflow. Upload a short dot wave sample of someone's voice. Then enter the sentence you want the cloned voice to say, and the system generates a new audio clip that mimics the original speaker. Under the hood, the project runs Lux TS, a lightweight neural texttospech model built on a zip voice architecture designed for highquality cloning while remaining extremely efficient. The model can produce clear 48 clerertz speech and generate audio at speeds exceeding 150 pex real time while using less than 1 gigabyte of GPU memory. This combination of quality and efficiency makes it attractive for developers experimenting with voice agents, storytelling tools, or personalized assistants. For example, a game developer could clone a character's voice once and automatically generate hundreds of dialogue lines without hiring additional voice actors. The space offers an easy way to test how fast realistic voice cloning is becoming in modern generative audio AI. Thanks for watching and exploring these hugging face spaces with me. title: Mac Mini Agents: OpenClaw is a NIGHTMARE... Use these SKILLS instead
description:
OpenClaw agents are a SECURITY NIGHTMARE. It's time to rip out the core of what makes claw agents great and ditch everything that makes them dangerous.

🎥 VIDEO REFERENCES
• Mac Mini Agent: https://github.com/disler/mac-mini-agent
• Stripe Minions Video: https://youtu.be/V5A1IU8VVp4
• Multi-Agent Observability Video: https://youtu.be/RpUTF_U4kiw
• New Apple Devices (Neo, Air, Pro): https://www.apple.com/newsroom/
• NanoClaw: https://nanoclaw.dev/
• Karpathy on Claw Security: https://x.com/karpathy/status/2024987174077432126

🚀 PUSH YOUR AGENTIC ENGINEERING FURTHER BEYOND
Tactical Agentic Coding: https://agenticengineer.com/tactical-agentic-coding?y=LOazLNQnB80

🔥 In this video, we break down exactly why OpenClaw and claw agents are an absolute disaster for engineers and vibe coders alike, and show you a safer, more professional way to build autonomous agents on your own Mac mini agent. Instead of generating vulnerable slop code at scale, we focus on just two skills and two CLI tools to give your AI agents full control over macOS automation, from terminal to GUI.

🛠️ Watch as we demonstrate a mac mini agent operating a complete macOS device end to end, fully autonomously. Using the steer skill for GUI control and the drive skill for terminal control via tmux, our Claude Code agent navigates apps, writes code, takes screenshots for proof of work, and even airdrops the results back to us. This is the real power of autonomous agents without the security nightmares of open claw.

🚀 We rip apart the architecture piece by piece: a listen HTTP server for the trigger layer, a direct CLI for firing off jobs, the steer application built in Swift for macOS automation, and the drive application for spinning up tmux terminals. It's agentic engineering done right. No bloated claw installs. No reckless package management. No prompt injection vulnerabilities. Just clean, minimal, professional agent architecture.

💡 The big idea here is simple: when you increase your agent's autonomy, you increase your own. But autonomy without understanding is vibe coding at its worst. Agentic engineering is knowing what your agents are doing so well you don't have to look. Whether you're running Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or your own custom agent harness, this video gives you the blueprint to steer and drive your own dedicated agent devices.

🌟 Key takeaways:

Mac Mini Agent: Deploy autonomous agents across any macOS device with a minimal architecture
Steer + Drive: Two skills that unlock full GUI and terminal control for your AI agents
Claw Agents Done Right: Extract the power of open claw without the security risks
YAML Job System: Scale to multiple macOS devices with a simple job management layer
Proof of Work: Teach your agents to verify and document everything they do

Stay focused and keep building.

#macos #aiagents #agenticengineering
