title: GitHub Trending Today #26: TerraInk, engram, Overstory, Open Terminal, discrawl, kula, rustc-php
description:
It is time for GitHub Trending Today, episode number 26. Today we have 34 trending open-source projects on GitHub.

Paid channel subscription: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC9Rrud-8CaHokDtK9FszvRg/join 

Text version link: https://githubawesome.com/github-trending-today-26/

00:00 - Introduction
00:14 - TerraInk https://github.com/yousifamanuel/terraink
00:40 - engram https://github.com/Gentleman-Programming/engram
01:04 - Overstory https://github.com/jayminwest/overstory
01:28 - Open Terminal https://github.com/open-webui/open-terminal
01:52 - jCodeMunch MCP https://github.com/jgravelle/jcodemunch-mcp
02:20 - autoresearch https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch
02:48 - Uncodixfy https://github.com/cyxzdev/Uncodixfy
03:14 - discrawl https://github.com/steipete/discrawl
03:39 - Antigravity Claw https://github.com/deborahikssv/Antigravity-claw
04:03 - Vibra Code https://github.com/sa4hnd/vibra-code
04:28 - kula https://github.com/c0m4r/kula
04:53 - rustc-php https://github.com/mrconter1/rustc-php
05:19 - claude-replay https://github.com/es617/claude-replay
05:43 - Symphony-ts https://github.com/OasAIStudio/symphony-ts
06:05 - DirPlayer https://github.com/igorlira/dirplayer-rs/
06:30 - Siftly https://github.com/viperrcrypto/Siftly
06:57 - WorldFM https://github.com/inspatio/worldfm
07:25 - chartli https://github.com/ahmadawais/chartli
07:49 - ClawPort https://github.com/JohnRiceML/clawport-ui
08:12 - AnsiSaver https://github.com/lardissone/ansi-saver
08:40 - Unredact https://github.com/Alex-Gilbert/unredact
09:05 - pdfdelta https://github.com/mli55/pdfdelta
09:30 - ClearDisk https://github.com/bysiber/cleardisk
09:53 - Wormhole https://github.com/MuhammadHananAsghar/wormhole
10:15 - distill https://github.com/samuelfaj/distill
10:40 - Agentlytics https://github.com/f/agentlytics
11:06 - SwiftUI-Agent-Skill https://github.com/twostraws/SwiftUI-Agent-Skill
11:31 - jackbutcher.md https://github.com/visualizevalue/jackbutcher.md
11:57 - Fonttrio https://github.com/kapishdima/fonttrio
12:18 - opusdelta https://github.com/OpusDelta/opusdelta
12:44 - RepoCheck https://github.com/WtxwNs/RepoCheck
13:09 - Motrix Next https://github.com/AnInsomniacy/motrix-next
13:33 - Arbor https://github.com/penso/arbor
13:59 - autoresearch-mlx https://github.com/trevin-creator/autoresearch-mlx

Thank you so much for watching. Please like the video and subscribe to our channel. 

#github #ai #opensource

▬▬▬▬▬▬ 👋 Contact me 👋 ▬▬▬▬▬▬
➡ LinkTree:   https://link.githubawesome.com/linktree
➡ Threads: https://link.githubawesome.com/threads
➡ Buy Me A Coffee https://link.githubawesome.com/buymeacoffee
transcript: Hey everyone, welcome back to GitHub awesome. It is time for GitHub trending today, episode number 26. Today we have 34 trending open source projects on GitHub. Let's get started. Ever wanted to make a custom map poster of your city? Terank is a map poster engine that runs entirely in your browser built with React that uses map liber and open street map data. You pick a location, customize colors, roads, water, parks, and typography, and export a print ready PNG. It's inspired by map to poster, but rebuilt from scratch with a full theme system and Docker support for self-hosting. If your AI coding agent keeps forgetting everything after its context gets compacted, engram is the fix. It gives your AI persistent memory using a single Go binary with SQLite and FTS5. The agent proactively saves its state after major architecture decisions or bug fixes and can instantly recover its full session state when needed. No more repeating yourself. Want to run an entire team of AI agents from a single coding session? Overstory does exactly that. It spawns worker agents in isolated git work trees using t-mucks, coordinates them through a lightning fast SQLite mail system, and safely merges their work back with tiered conflict resolution. supports Claude Code, PI, and Gemini CLI out of the box. One session, a whole swarm from the Open Web UI team. Open terminal. AI assistants are great at writing code, but they need somewhere to actually run it. Open terminal gives your AI a real sandboxed operating system via Docker. Instead of just writing text, your LLM gets full shell access to install software, run Python scripts, and manage files, all completely isolated from your host machine. Stop dumping massive 800 line files into your AI's context window. J-Code Munch is a brilliant new MCP server that indexes your entire codebase once using tree sitter as parsing. Instead of forcing Claude or VS Code to read entire files, the AI can ask to retrieve exact functions or classes by their symbol. It drops context usage from 40,000 tokens down to just 200, making AI cheap and hyperefficient. Are you ready to hire a tireless AI researcher? Coming from the legendary Andre Carpathy, Auto Research is a fully automated AI lab in a few Python scripts. You point it to a model and go to bed. The AI autonomously modifies the training architecture, runs a 5-minute training loop on your GPU, checks the validation loss, and decides whether to keep or discard the changes. By morning, you wake up to a newly optimized model. If you use AI to build front-end apps, every interface comes out looking like the exact same generic template. Uncodex 5 fixes this with a brilliantly simple hack. It is a single markdown file packed with strict rules that forbid your AI from defaulting to cliche UI patterns. Just drop it into your prompt and it blocks the AI's bad habits, pushing it to generate genuinely professional, unique designs. Need to search your Discord server history without relying on Discord's broken built-in search? Descrawrol is a Go CLI that mirrors an entire Discord guild, channels, threads, and full message history into a local SQLite database with full text search. Tail live gateway events for real-time sync. Run raw SQL queries against your archive or enable open AI embeddings for semantic search. Anti-gravity claw makes any web page defy gravity. pointed at a URL and this Rust physics engine wrapped in a Python CLI fetches the DOM, extracts every element, runs a real Verlet integration simulation with collision detection and outputs an interactive HTML animation you can open in any browser. Classic Google Gravity Vibes or enable Chaos Mode for maximum entropy. Think lovable or bolt new but for mobile and fully open source. Viber code lets you describe a React Native app in English. Then claude code running inside an E2B cloud sandbox generates it while you watch the preview update live on your actual phone. 60fps iOS chat UI backend on nex.js convex and ingest. The complete source code is right there to fork and self-host. Kula is a self-contained Linux server monitoring tool that reads directly from proc and cis every second. Single Go binary, zero external dependencies, no databases. Tracks per core CPU, memory, swap, network throughput, dis IO, process states, and clock sync, storing everything in a tiered ring buffer with bounded disc usage. Serves a real-time web dashboard and terminal boy. Get ready for the most cursed and brilliant project of the week, a Rust compiler written entirely in PHP. Rust CPHP takes raw Rust source code and directly emits x8664 Linux binaries with no LLVM, no assembler and no linker required. In just 2500 lines of PHP, it actually implements ownership checking, borrow checking, and mutable references. Absolutely unhinged project. This CLI tool takes the raw JSON log files from your cloud code sessions and instantly converts them into a beautiful interactive video-like HTML replay. You can step through the AI's execution, expand its tool calls, and see exactly what files it touched. It generates a single HTML file, making it the ultimate way to share complex AI debugging sessions with your team. When OpenAI dropped Symphfony, everyone was blown away. But it was written in Elixir. Symphfony Tease is the community's answer. A complete TypeScript port bringing the exact same autonomous worker orchestration, CI checking, and PR generation into the familiar Node.js ecosystem. All the power now in a stack you can actually hack on yourself. When browsers killed plugins, thousands of early 2000s web games were lost forever. Dire player brings them back. This team reverse engineered Macromedia Shockwave and rebuilt the entire engine from scratch in Rust and Web Assembly. No GC pauses, full memory safety, hardware accelerated graphics. Classics like Habbo Hotel and Lego Junkbot playable natively in your browser again. Your Twitter bookmarks are a black hole. Thousands of saved tweets you never look at again. Sifly fixes that. It's a local first bookmark organizer built in Typescript that uses AI to automatically categorize and tag every saved tweet, then generates gorgeous interactive mindmap visualizations. So you can actually explore your saved knowledge visually. Your data stays local. Your chaos gets structured. World FM is a real-time multiv- view diffusion model. Unlike AI video generators that just morph pixels, this is an open-source generative frame model designed specifically for spatial intelligence. Give it a single photo or text prompt and it generates a consistent interactive 3D world you can actually walk around in. Best of all, it is highly optimized to run in real time on consumer hardware like an RTX 4090. If you love building terminal based dashboards or CLI utilities, this is a gorgeous charting tool. Chartly allows you to render beautiful bar charts, line graphs, and visual data directly in your console stood out. Incredibly lightweight, makes your no.js scripts look intensely professional. There's even an agent skill so you can wire it directly into clawed code or codecs. Managing one AI agent is fun. Managing 20 from your terminal is chaos. Clawport fixes that. It's an open- source command center for orchestrating fleets of AI agents. Instead of digging through endless terminal logs, you get a sleek web UI to monitor, track, and control your entire autonomous workforce in real time. One command install via npm. Pure nostalgia. Ancesaver is a Mac OS screen saver that pulls an art directly from 16 colo. RS, the largest BBS art archive on the internet, and scrolls it across your screen at 60fps. Authentic CP437 fonts, neon color paletts, rendered with the same Leban library the archive uses, like leaving your terminal connected to a BBS you never logged off from. Scroll speed, scale, pack URLs, all configurable. Unredact is a computer vision tool that literally unmasks redacted names in classified documents. It analyzes the exact geometry of black redaction boxes, measuring font spacing, character width, and surrounding typography, then cross references known data sets to calculate exactly which names physically fit the space. The author is very clear. This is for research and entertainment only. Ever tried tracking down exactly what changed between two 50-page PDFs? It's a nightmare. PDF Delta fixes that. A lightning fast diffing engine built for PDFs. Instead of parsing raw text, it intelligently highlights the exact visual, structural, and textual differences between two files. Legal contracts, academic papers, design specs, hours of manual proof reading, gone. If you develop on a Mac, you probably have 50 gigabytes of garbage silently eating your drive right now. Clear disk hunts it down. It's a menu bar app built in Swift UI that scans for Xcode derived data, bloated node modules, forgotten Docker images, and old cocoa pods. Shows risk levels before you delete anything so you don't nuke active projects. Stop paying for Enro just to test a web hook. Wormhole is an open source alternative built in Go. Single command, any local port exposed to the internet with HTTPS by default. No signup, no config files, free custom subdomains out of the box. Built-in traffic inspector with request replay and HAR export for debugging routes through Cloudflare workers. If your AI coding agent reads 10,000 lines of test output, you're paying for every single token. Distill fixes that. Pipe any terminal command through it and a small local model summarizes the raw output down to just the signal your LLM actually needs. All tests passed. That's it. Up to 99% token savings and no more massive logs blowing out your context window. Ever wonder exactly how much you're relying on AI to write your code? Agent Litics tells you. It is a local analytics dashboard that reads the local chat history from your AI coding editors like cursor, windsurf, clawed code. Just run npx agent litics and it instantly generates beautiful activity heat maps, token usage stats and tool call frequencies directly from your local SQLite databases. If you use AI to build iOS apps, you know LLM constantly hallucinate deprecated Apple APIs. Paul Hudson just dropped the fix. a massive, highly optimized Swift UI agent skill for claude code or codecs. Load it in and it feeds your AI the latest Swift best practices, accessibility standards, and performance layout tips, so your agent writes productionready modern iOS code every single time. JackbuterMD is a single markdown file distilled from 50,000 of Jack Butcher's tweets by the Visualize Value team. Paste it into any AI conversation and the model instantly writes like him. Minimal, punchy, apheristic, concept driven. A complete personality transfer compressed into one file. Use it for your own writing or study it to understand how AI voice profiles actually work. Struggling to find the perfect font combinations for your UI? Font trio is absolute magic. It instantly generates gorgeous, highly legible three font pairings for your headings, subheadings, and body text all at once. No more guesswork. No more tab switching between Google fonts. Production ready typography in seconds. Opus Delta. The creators describe it as the first expression of machine emotion. A living delta of perpetual evolution. Instead of another chatbot, it attempts to simulate a genuinely evolving emotional state for AI, shifting its internal state based on interactions over time. Highly poetic, deeply experimental. If you're fascinated by machine consciousness and AI art, this one is mindbending. Ever stumbled on an open-source repo only to realize the license is locked down, the maintainers are ghosts, or it's just a SAS marketing funnel? Repo check audits that for you. It automatically scans licenses, contribution graphs, and repository metadata to give you a clear, unbiased health check on any project before you build a dependency on it. Must have for open source compliance. If you use Motrix, you know the original has been basically abandoned since 2023, stuck on aging Electron and Vue 2. Motrix next is the complete modernized rewrite. Same beautiful ad-free interface and powerful area 2 multi-threaded download engine rebuilt entirely on Tori 2 and Vue3. Faster, actively maintained and no electron memory bloat. AI agents like Claude Code struggle to understand how everything connects in code bases. Arbor fixes that. It maps your entire project into a dependency graph and exposes it directly to your LLM via MCP. Instead of blindly searching through text files, your agent gets structural graph level understanding of your codebase, enabling massive crossfile refactoring with zero hallucinations. We covered Carpathy's auto research back in project 6, the autonomous AI research lab that runs while you sleep. Auto research MLX brings it natively to Apple Silicon. It strips out PyTorch and CUDA entirely, rebuilding the training loop on Apple's MLX framework. Now your Mac can autonomously design, train, and benchmark neural network architectures every 5 minutes using 100% of its unified memory and GPU.

transcript: Every week, developers release new tools, frameworks, and experiments as open-source projects on GitHub. In this weekly update, we review top trending open-source GitHub projects this week, so developers can quickly find useful tools to explore. These projects include AI agent frameworks, developer utilities, distributed systems, and productivity tools. Without wasting time, let's get started. >> Before we jump into today's project updates, here's a quick announcement for everyone. We've launched a brand new YouTube channel called AI Agent Studio dedicated entirely to AI Agent projects, tutorials, and tools. So, if you're interested in staying up to date with the latest AI agent open source projects, learning how to build your own agents, or exploring cuttingedge agent frameworks, make sure to check it out. Subscribe now to get weekly videos, in-depth guides, and real time project breakdowns. The link is right there in the description. Don't miss it. All right, let's get into today's video. >> Project number one, Aerial Reinforcement Learning System for training AI agents. Aerial is an open-source reinforcement learning framework designed to train large language model agents through environment interaction and feedback. The system provides infrastructure for running RL training loops where agents generate actions, receive rewards, and improve behavior over time. It supports scalable distributed training and integrates with modern LLM workflows so developers can experiment with agent learning beyond static prompting. The framework manages rollout collection, evaluation, and policy updates while coordinating compute resources. This helps researchers and developers build and study learning based AI agents. Explore the repository to experiment with reinforcement learning for LLM agents. Project number two, Reine, memory system for longrunning AI agents. Reme is a memory framework designed to help AI agents manage long-term context across tasks and conversations. The system organizes information into structured memory that agents can store, retrieve, and update during execution. It provides mechanisms for deciding what information should persist and how it should be recalled during reasoning. Developers integrate the memory layer with agent frameworks so models can maintain knowledge across sessions rather than operating statelessly. This supports complex workflows where agents need to remember prior actions or results. Explore the repository to add persistent memory to your AI agent systems. Project number three, Hive Core cryptographic library for hidden vector encryption. Hive core is a cryptographic library from Microsoft that implements hidden vector encryption, a technique that enables encrypted data queries without revealing the query itself. The project provides core algorithms and utilities for encrypting attributes and evaluating secure predicates over that encrypted data. Developers can use the library to build systems where sensitive information remains encrypted while still supporting controlled search operations. The implementation focuses on correctness and performance for research and applied cryptography experiments. This helps engineers explore privacy preserving data access techniques. Explore the repository to experiment with hidden vector encryption primitives. Project number four, agent skills for context engineering. Skill library for structured AI prompts. Agent skills for context engineering is a repository that provides reusable agent skill definitions designed to structure prompts and context handling for AI systems. Each skill describes how an agent should approach a specific reasoning or workflow task, helping guide model behavior through clear contextual instructions. Developers can combine these skills to construct more reliable agent workflows and maintain consistent context management. The repository acts as a catalog of patterns for building structured prompt pipelines. This helps engineers improve control over AI agent reasoning and execution. Explore the repository to apply context engineering patterns in your agent systems. Project number five, Pyono. Monorreo for building PI ecosystem projects. Pyono is a monorrepo that organizes the development environment and shared infrastructure for projects in the pi ecosystem created by the bad logic team. The repository centralizes multiple related components in one workspace so developers can build, test and manage them together. Shared tooling, build configuration and project structure simplify collaboration across different modules. The monorrepo approach keeps dependencies consistent and improves maintainability across the ecosystem. Developers working with Pi projects can use this repository as the base development environment. Explore the repository to understand and build projects inside the PI monorrepo workspace. Project number six, Open Viking distributed system for training large models. Open Viking is an open-source distributed training framework designed to support large-scale machine learning workloads. The system provides infrastructure for coordinating compute nodes, managing training tasks, and scaling model training across distributed hardware. It focuses on efficient resource management and flexible deployment environments so teams can run training workloads in different infrastructures. Developers integrate their model training pipelines into the system to orchestrate distributed execution. This supports experiments and production workflows that require scalable compute coordination. Explore the repository to run distributed training workloads with Open Viking. Project number seven, skills tool interface library for AI assistants. Skills is a repository from OpenAI that defines reusable tool interfaces that AI assistants can call during reasoning and task execution. Each skill represents a structured capability such as interacting with external tools or performing defined operations. Developers integrate these skills into agent systems so models can trigger specific actions instead of generating unstructured responses. The project demonstrates patterns for connecting AI models with deterministic tool execution. This helps developers design assistants that reliably perform tasks through structured capabilities. Explore the repository to integrate tool-based skills into your AI agents. Project number eight, finicki Mac OS browser routing configuration tool. Finicki is a Mac OS application that lets users control which browser opens when clicking different links. The tool intercepts URL open events and applies userdefined rules written in JavaScript to decide which browser should handle the request. Developers can route links based on domains, URL patterns or other conditions. This makes it easy to send work links to one browser and personal links to another. The configuration file defines routing logic that FIN evaluates whenever a link opens. Explore the repository to customize browser routing behavior on Mac OS. Project number nine, Olivin web interface for running server commands. Olivin is a self-hosted web interface that allows users to run predefined commands and scripts on a server through a simple dashboard. The system connects a web UI with back-end command execution so administrators can trigger tasks without using a terminal. Configuration files define available actions and in the commands they run on the host system. This allows teams to expose safe operational tasks through a browser interface. It runs locally on servers and focuses on simple deployment and management. Explore the repository to create a web dashboard for server operations. Project number 10. Undrift tool for managing AI prompt drift. Undrift is a development tool that helps teams track and manage prompt drift in AI applications. The system compares prompt versions and outputs to detect changes that affect model behavior over time. Developers can monitor how prompts evolve and ensure that updates do not unintentionally degrade results. The repository provides utilities for organizing prompts, running evaluations, and identifying differences across versions. This helps teams maintain consistent AI performance as prompts change. Explore the repository to monitor and control prompt drift in your AI workflows. Project number 11, FAPHCHA, proofof work capture system. FSAP is an open- source capture system that uses proofof work challenges instead of traditional visual puzzles. When a user interacts with a protected resource, the client performs a small computational task that verifies the request is legitimate. The system aims to reduce spam and automated abuse while avoiding image recognition puzzles that rely on human solving. Developers integrate the challenge mechanism into web applications to validate requests before allowing access. The design focuses on simplicity and automation resistance. Explore the repository to add proofof workbased capture protection to your application. Project number 12, Rocket Chat, self-hosted team communication platform. Rocket Chat is an open-source collaboration platform that provides real-time messaging, file sharing, and team communication features. The system runs on self-hosted infrastructure and offers web, desktop, and mobile interfaces for users. It supports channels, direct messaging, integrations, and automation through APIs and apps. Organizations deploy it to maintain full control over data and infrastructure while enabling modern team communication workflows. Developers can extend the platform through plugins and integrations with other services. Explore the repository to deploy and customize your own team communication system. Project number 13. Agent relay communication layer for multi- aent systems. Agent relay is a framework that provides a messaging and coordination layer for AI agents working together in distributed systems. The project defines protocols and infrastructure that allow agents to send tasks, share context, and exchange results. It focuses on enabling collaboration between multiple agents running in different environments or services. Developers integrate the relay layer with their agent logic so agents can coordinate actions across workflows. This supports complex systems where specialized agents handle different responsibilities. Explore the repository to build distributed multi-agent communication systems. Project number 14, component gallery. Curated gallery of UI components. Component gallery is a repository that collects and showcases reusable user interface components for web development. The project organizes components into a browsable gallery where developers can explore designs and implementation patterns. Each entry demonstrates how a component behaves and how it can be integrated into applications. The gallery acts as a reference for developers building interfaces who want examples of common UI elements. It focuses on practical components that can be reused or adapted in real projects. Explore the repository to browse and experiment with the component gallery. Project number 15, Cute Ghosty theme and customization for Ghosty terminal. Cute Ghosty is a customization project that provides themes and visual styling for the Ghosty Terminal emulator. The repository includes configuration files and assets that modify the terminals appearance with custom colors and interface elements. Users apply the configuration to change the look and feel of the ghosty environment. The project focuses on aesthetic customization rather than altering terminal functionality. Developers and terminal users can adapt the configuration to personalize their workflow environment. Explore the repository to customize the appearance of your ghosty terminal. Project number 16. Plate. Rich text editor framework for React. Plate is a rich text editor framework built for React that provides a modular system for creating customizable editing experiences. It extends the Slate editor architecture with plugins that add features such as formatting, tables, media embedding, and collaboration tools. Developers compose plugins to build tailored editors for applications such as note systems, document editors, and publishing tools. The framework exposes APIs and components that integrate directly into React projects. This allows teams to build advanced editing interfaces with structured configuration. Explore the repository to build customrich text editors with React. Project number 17. Debug Swift. Swift debugging utilities for developers. Debug Swift is a collection of debugging utilities designed to help developers inspect and analyze Swift applications during development. The project provides tools and helpers that expose useful runtime information and simplify debugging workflows. Developers integrate the utilities into Swift projects to improve visibility into program behavior while testing or troubleshooting. The focus is on making debugging tasks easier within the Swift development environment. This helps engineers understand application state and diagnose issues more effectively. Explore the repository to add debugging tools to your Swift development workflow. Project number 18, Portless tool for running apps without fixed ports. Portless is an experimental development tool from Versal Labs that allows applications to run without manually assigning network ports. The system manages connections and routing so services can start without port conflicts in local environments. Developers run applications normally while the tool handles how traffic reaches each service. This simplifies development workflows where multiple apps run simultaneously on the same machine. The project explores alternative approaches to local service networking and routing. Explore the repository to experiment with portless application execution. Project number 19, CAP. Open-source screen recording and sharing tool. CAP is an open-source screen recording tool designed for quickly capturing and sharing screen recordings. The application records the screen, microphone, or camera and generates sharable links for the captured content. It provides a lightweight interface for recording short demos, tutorials, or feedback clips. The project focuses on fast capture and easy sharing without complex editing workflows. Developers can run and extend the application using the open codebase. Explore the repository to record and share screen captures with CAP. Project number 20, Sound Sphere command line soundboard tool. Sound Sphere is a command line soundboard that allows users to play predefined audio clips directly from the terminal. The tool loads sound files and maps them to commands that trigger playback when executed. Users can configure their own set of sounds and trigger them during streams, calls, or events. The application runs locally and focuses on simple command-driven audio playback. Developers and terminal users can extend it by adding new sound files or command mappings. Explore the repository to create a customizable terminal soundboard. Thanks for watching. See you in the next update.

The repository provides utilities for organizing prompts, running evaluations, and identifying differences across versions. This helps teams maintain consistent AI performance as prompts change.

transcript: Every week, developers release useful open-source tools and frameworks on GitHub. In this weekly update, we review top trending open source GitHub projects this week so developers can quickly discover practical tools worth exploring. These projects cover AI agents, automation systems, CLI utilities, and developer workflows. Without wasting time, let's get started. Before we jump into today's project updates, here's a quick announcement for everyone. We've launched a brand new YouTube channel called AI Agent Studio dedicated entirely to AI Agent projects, tutorials, and tools. So, if you're interested in staying up to date with the latest AI agent open source projects, learning how to build your own agents, or exploring cuttingedge agent frameworks, make sure to check it out. Subscribe now to get weekly videos, in-depth guides, and real time project breakdowns. The link is right there in the description. Don't miss it. All right, let's get into today's video. >> Project number one, Agency Agents library of specialized AI role prompts. Agency agents is an open-source collection of AI agent definitions that developers can use to simulate a full team of specialized experts inside an LLM workflow. The project provides dozens of agent profiles, each defined with a clear identity, responsibilities, workflows, communication style, and expected deliverables. So, an AI model can operate in a focused role rather than as a generic assistant. Developers can copy these agent files directly into environments such as clawed code or use them as reference templates. Each agent guides how the model plans tasks and produces outputs like code, designs, or strategies. Explore the agents and integrate the roles into your AI workflows. Project number two, SEO machine. Claude Workspace for automated SEO content workflows. SEO machine is an AI development workspace built for claude code that helps teams research, write, analyze, and optimize long- form SEO content inside a structured workflow. The project organizes files for topics, research, drafts, and publishing while providing custom commands such as research, writing, rewriting, and analysis and optimization. It connects with tools like Google Analytics, Google Search Console, and data for SEO to analyze search performance and competitors. Context files define brand voice, style, and keywords. So, the model produces consistent content. This helps developers and marketing teams automate SEO content pipelines. Explore the repository to build your own AI assisted content workflow. Project number three, money printer v2 automation toolkit for online revenue workflows. Money printer v2 is a Python automation tool that coordinates multiple workflows designed to generate online revenue through automated social media and outreach systems. The project includes modules for running a Twitter bot, generating YouTube shorts, managing affiliate marketing campaigns, and discovering local businesses for cold outreach. Auler runs these actions automatically through recurring jobs, while the modular architecture allows developers to extend or customize automation tasks. The system runs locally with Python and integrates scripts and services for publishing and promotion workflows. Developers can modify the modules to experiment with automated marketing pipelines. Explore the repository and adapt the automation system to your own workflows. Project number four, RU View, Wi-Fi based human pose sensing without cameras. Ru View is an experimental sensing system that uses Wi-Fi signals to estimate human pose and movement without relying on cameras. The project processes wireless signal data and applies machine learning techniques to reconstruct body pose, detect presence, and estimate physiological signals such as breathing. By analyzing changes in signal reflections from commodity Wi-Fi devices, the system converts radio data into spatial human activity information. This approach enables privacy preserving sensing in environments where cameras are not suitable. Developers interested in wireless sensing, AI perception, and ambient intelligence can explore the repository to experiment with camera-free human activity detection. Project number five, Rofflow multi-agent orchestration platform for clawed workflows. RuFlow is an open-source agent orchestration platform designed for building and coordinating multi-agent AI systems. The framework manages groups of agents that collaborate on tasks and execute structured workflows, allowing developers to run distributed reasoning and automation processes. It integrates with environments such as claude code and other AI coding systems and supports retrieval augmented workflows and distributed coordination patterns. The platform focuses on swarm style execution where multiple agents communicate and handle different stages of a process. Developers building complex AI assistants or automated workflows can use Rofflow to manage agent collaboration and execution pipelines. Project number six, Superpowers AI capability layer for developer workflows. Superpowers is an open-source developer tool that provides a structured way to extend AI assistance with specialized capabilities. The project defines reusable capability modules that an AI system can invoke to perform specific tasks, allowing developers to add domain knowledge and controlled behaviors to AI workflows. These modules act as a capability layer between the model and the developer environment, enabling more reliable actions and structured outputs. By defining clear tools and instructions, developers can guide AI assistance toward consistent execution. Explore the repository to add modular capabilities to your own AIdriven development workflows. Project number seven, Open Claw. Open implementation of the claw AI system. Open Claw is an open-source project that implements the claw system for building agentic AI environments that can interact with software and perform complex tasks. The project focuses on creating agents that perceive an environment, reason about actions, and execute operations across software tools. It provides a foundation for experimenting with autonomous workflows where models analyze inputs, decide on actions, and operate within a controlled runtime. Developers can use the project to study agent behavior and build custom task execution systems. Explore the repository to experiment with open agent execution environments. Project number eight, hugging face skills. Skill library for AI assistants and agents. Hugging face skills is an open repository that provides reusable skills designed for AI assistance in agent systems. Each skill defines a specific capability that an agent can perform, such as interacting with tools, retrieving information, or executing structured tasks. Developers can plug these skills into agent frameworks to extend what an AI system can do without rewriting logic from scratch. The repository serves as a shared capability layer where contributors add new skills that agents can invoke during reasoning or execution. Explore the repository to integrate reusable capabilities into your own AI agent systems. Project number nine, Heretic terminal-based Git repository management tool. Heretic is a command line tool designed to help developers manage and inspect Git repositories directly from the terminal. The project provides commands for exploring repository history, working with branches, and navigating project structure without leaving the CLI environment. It focuses on fast navigation and simplified workflows so developers can analyze repositories and perform common Git tasks efficiently by combining repository inspection with terminal usability. The tool supports developers who prefer keyboard-driven workflows. Explore the repository to streamline git exploration and management in your terminal environment. Project number 10. Goggly command line client for the GOG platform. Goggly is a command line client that allows users to interact with the GOG gaming platform directly from the terminal. The tool enables operations such as listing owned games, downloading installers, and managing game files without using the graphical launcher. It communicates with GOG services through authenticated requests and organizes downloads locally on the user's machine. Developers and Linux users can integrate it into scripts or automated environments where GOG cli launchers are not practical. Explore the repository to manage your GOG library from the command line. Project number 11, PM skills structured skill catalog for product managers. PM Skills is an open-source knowledge base that organizes the core skills required for modern product management. The repository defines skills across areas such as strategy, discovery, delivery, leadership, and analytics with clear descriptions of what each skill involves and how it applies in real work. The structure helps teams understand capability gaps, guide hiring, and support professional development for product managers. Everything lives as simple documentation that can be browsed, extended, or adapted by teams. This helps PMs and organizations create a shared framework for evaluating product skills. Explore the repository to review the full product management skill map. Project number 12, GWS, command line interface for Google Workspace. GWS is an open-source CLI built by Google Workspace that allows developers and administrators to manage Workspace services directly from the terminal. The tool connects with Google Workspace APIs and provides commands for interacting with services such as Gmail, Drive, and other Workspace resources. Authentication uses Google Cloud credentials and commands execute actions or retrieve data through API calls. This makes it easier to automate administrative workflows, scripts, and integrations without using the web interface. Developers and IT teams can use the CLI to manage workspace environments programmatically. Explore the repository to run workspace operations from your terminal. Project number 13. YT browse terminal interface for browsing YouTube content. YT browse is a terminal-based application that allows users to browse and explore YouTube content from the command line. The tool fetches data from YouTube and renders search results, channels, playlists, and video metadata inside an interactive terminal interface. Navigation works with keyboard controls, allowing users to move through results, inspect videos, and open playback links without leaving the CLI environment. The project focuses on lightweight browsing and discovery for users who prefer terminal workflows. Developers and terminal enthusiasts can run it locally to explore video content without using a browser. Explore the repository to browse YouTube directly from your terminal. Project number 14. Agents framework for building realtime voice AI agents. Agents is an open- source framework from LiveKit for building AI agents that communicate through real-time voice, video, and data streams. The system connects language models, speechtoext, texttospech, and external tools into a structured agent pipeline that runs on LiveKit's real-time infrastructure. Developers define agent behavior and code and connect the agent to live audio sessions where it listens, reasons, and responds in conversation. The framework supports integrations with common LLM providers and runs in server environments that manage streaming communication. This helps developers build interactive voice assistants and conversational AI systems. Explore the repository to start building real-time AI agents. Project number 15, terminal tool for voice conversations with AI. Schwo is a command line application that enables voice conversations with large language models directly from the terminal. The tool records audio from the microphone, converts speech to text, sends the text to an AI model, and returns responses using textto-spech playback. It integrates speech recognition and synthesis services to create a continuous voice interaction loop. Users run the program locally and speak naturally while the model processes the conversation in real time. This gives developers a lightweight way to experiment with voice interfaces without building a full application. Explore the repository to run voice AI interactions from your terminal. Project number 16. Spank simple task runner and command automation tool. Spank is a lightweight command automation tool that helps developers define and run repeatable tasks from configuration files. The project allows users to group commands into named tasks that execute from the command line, similar to a build or workflow runner. Tasks can call shell commands and other tasks, making it easier to automate development steps such as builds, tests, or deployments. Configuration files keep automation steps organized inside a project repository. Developers who prefer simple CLI automation can use it to replace custom shell scripts. Explore the repository to organize and run project tasks with a single command. Project number 17, Benchmark. Benchmark for detecting AI hallucinations.  benchmark is an evaluation data set and testing framework designed to measure how language models handle misleading or incorrect prompts. The repository provides structured questions where the correct behavior is to reject the premise rather than produce a fabricated answer. Models are tested on whether they detect the flawed assumption and respond with clarification instead of hallucinating information. The benchmark helps researchers analyze reliability and reasoning behavior in language models. Everything runs as a reproducible evaluation setup that developers can apply to different models. Explore the repository to test how models respond to misleading prompts. Project number 18, Liissium framework for building AI applications with memory. Liissium is an open-source framework for building AI applications that maintain structured memory across interactions. The system provides tools for storing, retrieving, and updating contextual information so an AI application can remember past conversations and data. Developers define memory structures and retrieval logic that integrate with language model workflows. The framework manages how information is stored and injected back into prompts during execution. This helps developers create assistance and applications that maintain continuity over time instead of responding statelessly. Explore the repository to build AI apps with persistent memory. Project number 19, Fix EO tool for auditing and improving website SEO. Fix AO is a command line tool that analyzes a website and identifies issues affecting search engine optimization. The program scans pages, evaluates metadata, structure, and content signals and reports problems that may reduce search visibility. It runs locally and processes website data to produce actionable diagnostics that developers can review and fix. The goal is to provide a simple developer focused way to audit SEO without relying on complex web dashboards. This helps engineers quickly detect optimization issues during development or deployment. Explore the repository to audit your site's SEO directly from the command line. Project number 20. Corridor Key. Open hardware system for secure digital keys. Corridor Key is an open hardware and software project that explores secure digital key systems for physical access control. The repository documents hardware designs and supporting software used to authenticate and manage digital credentials that unlock doors or access points. The system focuses on cryptographic a verification and device communication to confirm authorized access. Developers and hardware builders can study the design and experiment with building compatible key devices or readers. This provides a foundation for exploring open access control systems and digital credential security. Explore the repository to understand and experiment with the corridor key system. Thanks for watching.

products.name: shuo
products.synopsis: sub-500ms latency phone agent orchestration
products.transcript_text: Project number 13. YT browse terminal interface for browsing YouTube content. YT browse is a terminal-based application that allows users to browse and explore YouTube content from the command line. The tool fetches data from YouTube and renders search results, channels, playlists, and video metadata inside an interactive terminal interface. Navigation works with keyboard controls, allowing users to move through results, inspect videos, and open playback links without leaving the CLI environment. The project focuses on lightweight browsing and discovery for users who prefer terminal workflows. Developers and terminal enthusiasts can run it locally to explore video content without using a browser.

products.name: spank
products.synopsis: Spank simple task runner and command automation tool. Spank is a lightweight command automation tool that helps developers define and run repeatable tasks from configuration files
products.transcript_text: Developers and terminal enthusiasts can run it locally to explore video content without using a browser. Explore the repository to browse YouTube directly from your terminal. Project number 14. Agents framework for building realtime voice AI agents. Agents is an open- source framework from LiveKit for building AI agents that communicate through real-time voice, video, and data streams. The system connects language models, speechtoext, texttospech, and external tools into a structured agent pipeline that runs on LiveKit's real-time infrastructure. Developers define agent behavior and code and connect the agent to live audio sessions where it listens, reasons, and responds in conversation.

products.name: BullshitBench v2
products.synopsis: BullshitBench measures whether AI models challenge nonsensical prompts instead of confidently answering them, created by Peter Gostev.
products.transcript_text: Developers define agent behavior and code and connect the agent to live audio sessions where it listens, reasons, and responds in conversation. The framework supports integrations with common LLM providers and runs in server environments that manage streaming communication. This helps developers build interactive voice assistants and conversational AI systems. Explore the repository to start building real-time AI agents. Project number 15, terminal tool for voice conversations with AI. Schwo is a command line application that enables voice conversations with large language models directly from the terminal. The tool records audio from the microphone, converts speech to text, sends the text to an AI model, and returns responses using textto-spech playback.

products.name: Lysium
products.synopsis: A cross-platform control plane for async, agent-driven software delivery.
products.transcript_text: The tool records audio from the microphone, converts speech to text, sends the text to an AI model, and returns responses using textto-spech playback. It integrates speech recognition and synthesis services to create a continuous voice interaction loop. Users run the program locally and speak naturally while the model processes the conversation in real time. This gives developers a lightweight way to experiment with voice interfaces without building a full application. Explore the repository to run voice AI interactions from your terminal. Project number 16. Spank simple task runner and command automation tool. Spank is a lightweight command automation tool that helps developers define and run repeatable tasks from configuration files.

products.name: fixseo
products.synopsis: CLI tool to help agents fix seo issues
products.transcript_text: Spank is a lightweight command automation tool that helps developers define and run repeatable tasks from configuration files. The project allows users to group commands into named tasks that execute from the command line, similar to a build or workflow runner. Tasks can call shell commands and other tasks, making it easier to automate development steps such as builds, tests, or deployments. Configuration files keep automation steps organized inside a project repository. Developers who prefer simple CLI automation can use it to replace custom shell scripts. Explore the repository to organize and run project tasks with a single command. Project number 17, Benchmark. Benchmark for detecting AI hallucinations. benchmark is an evaluation data set and testing framework designed to measure how language models handle misleading or incorrect prompts.

products.name: CorridorKey
products.synopsis: Perfect Green Screen Keys
products.transcript_text: benchmark is an evaluation data set and testing framework designed to measure how language models handle misleading or incorrect prompts. The repository provides structured questions where the correct behavior is to reject the premise rather than produce a fabricated answer. Models are tested on whether they detect the flawed assumption and respond with clarification instead of hallucinating information. The benchmark helps researchers analyze reliability and reasoning behavior in language models. Everything runs as a reproducible evaluation setup that developers can apply to different models. Explore the repository to test how models respond to misleading prompts.

transcript: In today's episode, GitHub Trending Weekly number 26, we are blasting through 32 trending open- source projects on GitHub. Let's get into it. Want AI code review without handing your proprietary code to a third party SAS? Open Review from Versel Labs is the answer. Deploy it to Versell, connect it as a GitHub app, and just mention at oopen review in any pull request. It spins up a sandboxed environment, clones your branch, and deploys a claude powered agent to review the diff. If it finds lint errors or formatting issues, it automatically commits and pushes the fixes directly to your PR branch. Ever wish your AI coding assistant could just draw you an architecture diagram? The Excaladraw diagram skill for claude code does exactly that. It generates beautiful structured Excaladraw JSON files directly from natural language. It doesn't just spit out random boxes. It creates meaningful visual arguments complete with system flows and brand aligned colors. A massive timesaver for documentation. SSD stands for speculative speculative decoding. Standard speculative decoding is fast, but it forces the fast model to wait for the slow model to verify tokens. SSD breaks that barrier by parallelizing the process across distinct hardware, literally predicting the verification outcomes before they even happen. The result, a lossless inference engine that runs up to 2x faster than the strongest baselines in the world. If you use clawed code, you know the terminal tells you almost nothing about what the AI is actually doing under the hood. Claude DevTools fixes this. It's a desktop app that reverse engineers the raw session logs already on your machine. Zero config, no API keys. It visualizes your entire hidden context window. Every file read, every tool call, recursive sub aent trees, and exactly where your tokens are going. OpenClaw master skills is a highly curated weekly updated directory over 127 of the absolute best OpenClaw skills aggregated from across the web. Instead of writing custom agent tools from scratch, you can just browse this directory, plug the skills directly into your setup, and give your AI agent instant superpowers like advanced repository analysis and document processing. Tired of doing 50 takes just to record the perfect browser demo? Webre from Versel Labs fixes that. Write a simple JSON config, define your clicks, typing, drag and drops, and pauses, and it automatically drives headless Chrome to record it for you. 60 frames per second encoded to MP4, WebM, or GIF via FFmpeg. It automatically adds animated cursors, keystroke overlay, HUDs, and sound effects. This is for the product managers out there. PM Skills is an open- source marketplace packed with over 100 agentic skills and commands built for claude code. It turns your AI into a dedicated PM assistant guiding you through workflows for product discovery, market research, go to market strategies, and analytics. It is basically an AI operating system for making better product decisions. If you build mobile web apps, you know they often lack that premium native feel. Web haptics fixes this instantly. It brings native feeling vibration feedback directly to the mobile web with simple hooks for react view and spelt. Just attach the trigger to your buttons and suddenly your PWA feels alive and tactile in your user's hands. It even includes a desktop debug mode with audio feedback. Writing skills for AI agents can get messy fast. Minko get brings us the definitive skills best practices repository. It's a master class in writing professional-grade agent skills that maintain a lean context window. It teaches you how to structure your directories, validate your skills using LLMs, and ensure your agent reliably triggers the right tools without hallucinating. If you are tired of babysitting your AI coding agents, Symfony turns your project management board into the orchestrator. It natively monitors your linear board, spawns an isolated workspace, and assigns an AI agent to build the feature autonomously. It even provides proof of work by running CI checks, handling PR reviews, and generating a video walkthrough before landing the PR. If Symphfony is your AI project manager, Paperclip is your entire corporate infrastructure. Build as the orchestration layer for a zero human company. This framework gives you a complete corporate architecture out of the box. You get dynamic org charts, budget controls, and companywide goal alignment. You can assign different AI models to act as different departments, letting them operate autonomously to run a full-blown AI business. If you are optimizing web performance, you need Splatash. It's a ridiculously lightweight alternative to blur hash and thumb hash. It encodes any image into exactly 16 bytes, allowing you to instantly generate a blurry 32x32 placeholder while the main image loads in the background. With implementations in Go, TypeScript, and Python, it is the ultimate tool for startups building massive userenerated content feeds. Google open-sourced the Google Workspace CLI written entirely in Rust. This CLI gives you complete access to Drive, Gmail, Calendar, and Docs. It was built natively for AI. It instantly spins up as an MCP server, meaning your local AI agent can now read your emails, check your calendar, and draft replies without you writing a single custom integration. What if AI could design your UI for you in real time? Open Pencil is an open-source AI native alternative to Figma. Instead of dragging boxes for hours, it runs an MCP server in the background. So you can open clawed code and literally type generate a rover management dashboard using the editorial style guide and watch the AI place and style components on your canvas live. Ships with a gallery of professional style guides. If you are tired of paying $100 a month for bloated tools like Semrush just to do basic keyword research, this is for you. It's an open-source self-hosted SEO tool powered by a pay-per- usage API. You get keyword tracking, competitor domain research, and unlimited free site audits in a gorgeous, clean interface, all for fractions of a penny per search. If you've ever watched Arsync crawl through a massive SSH transfer, Parsync is for you. It's a high throughput resumable sync tool built in Rust that pulls files from SSH remotes in parallel. R sync but with actual concurrency baked in pointed at a remote path. It pulls everything down with multiple simultaneous transfers and optional block delta sync for large files. If you want to master AI engineering, you need torch code. Think of this as leak code specifically for PyTorch. It provides a guided practice environment where you are tasked with implementing neural network operators and complex architectures entirely from scratch with automated grading and instant feedback. It is the absolute best way to deeply understand the math and code behind modern AI. Are you tired of your AI coding assistant generating generic UI components? UI design brain is a cursor skill that gives your AI agent actual design system knowledge. It feeds the agent best practices, layout patterns, and modern SAS conventions for over 60 interface components, ensuring that the code it spits out is production grade, accessible, and beautifully minimal. Word Doodle is a super fun front-end project. It's a browser-based generative engine that takes raw text and transforms it into dense handdrawn style typographic doodle art. Built purely in vanilla JavaScript, it uses frequency mapping and randomized collision detection to pack words onto a canvas as tightly as possible. Try pasting your project's readme into it. The results are mesmerizing. Synapse CAD is a desktop 3D CAD IDE built entirely in Rust that brings AI vibe coding to 3D modeling. It combines an OpenCAD code editor, a real-time 3D viewport, and an AI assistant. You can click on parts of your 3D model and use natural language to tell the AI to modify it and it writes the underlying parametric code for you. An incredible tool for 3D printing enthusiasts. If you are scaling up your AI workforce and moving to production, this tool lets you run an entire fleet of AI agents natively on Kubernetes. It acts as an orchestrator allowing you to administer your K cluster agentically. It beautifully bridges the gap between enterprisegrade Kubernetes infrastructure and autonomous AI swarms. If you love DevOps and AI, this is an absolute mustsee. Need to stress test your web apps? Kryo is a blistering fast HTTP benchmarking tool written entirely in the Crystal programming language. It is incredibly lightweight, super easy to install, and allows you to hammer your servers with thousands of requests to accurately measure performance. bottlenecks. If you love clean, fast CLI tools and need a modern alternative to Apache Bench, check this out. If you are running complex autonomous agents using Open Claw, you know exactly how painful it is to migrate or reset them. This utility gives you a seamless one-click backup and restore solution. It safely packages up your agents workspace, credentials, loaded skills, and full conversation history. It provides total peace of mind for serious agent developers. If AI writes the code, shouldn't the AI's thought process be part of the commit? Git Momento is a brilliant Git extension that tracks your clawed or codeex sessions and attaches them to your commits using Git notes. It saves a cleaned up markdown transcript of the AI session that produced the code, preserving the provenence and thought process for future maintainers without breaking your standard Git workflow. LTX Desktop is a video editor powered by LightRick's new LTX 2.3 multimodal model. It lets you generate and edit text to video and image to video entirely offline using your NVIDIA GPU. The timeline editor is insane. You can generate multiple takes per clip non-destructively. Use contextaware gap fill and regenerate specific sections of a video without leaving the timeline. Tired of importing a massive 33 kilob library just for scroll animations? React Kino does it in under 1 kilob. It's a cinematic scroll-driven storytelling library for React that relies purely on CSS sticky positioning and native compositing. Zero dependencies, buttery smooth Apple style scroll experiences, 12 declarative components, including parallax, video scroll, and text reveal. This is a massive open-source graph infrastructure originally built to map out complex Brazilian public records for transparency and intelligence work. Powered by a Neo Forj graph database and async Python fast API backend and a React 19 front end. It is designed to ingest massive data sets and connect the dots to find hidden investigative signals. Fractals is a recursive agentic task orchestrator. Give it a highle goal and it dynamically grows a self-similar tree of subtasks running each leaf in an isolated git work tree using a swarm of AI agents in parallel. Once the leaves are done, a merger agent combines branches bottom up, resolving conflicts until a single unified project emerges at the root. Incredibly clever architecture for massive AI workflows. Ready for the Truman Show of AI development? Yoyo Evolve started as a 200line coding agent written in Rust. Every eight hours, a GitHub action wakes it up. It reads its own source code, checks community issues, finds bugs, implements improvements, and runs tests. pass, it commits, fail, it reverts. No human writes its code. In just a few days, it autonomously refactored itself into over 1500 lines across multiple modules. Antenna, a web-based antenna simulator powered by the legendary NEC2 engine running entirely in your browser. The creator compiled Nest 2C directly to Web Assembly, so complex electromagnetic antenna simulations happen client side. No server works on your phone, too. Beautiful interactive 3D models, instant gain in DBI, elevation, and azimuth as you hover. Vibbma brings vibe coding directly to Figma. This MCP server allows your AI agents to design interfaces inside your actual Figma files. You can chat with Claude and tell it to read your current layout, create new components, or modify your design system styles. It bridges the gap between LLM reasoning and professional design software, turning your AI into a junior UI designer. Ghost is a project that turns Git itself into an AI coding agent. It introduces intentbased commits to your workflow. Instead of writing code and then writing a commit message, you just describe your intent and the agent natively interacts with your codebase to make the changes and log them seamlessly into your Git history. It bridges the gap between AI generation and version control. >> [music]
